summary: Gruber dismantles Dan Crow's Guardian article arguing that Apple has 'passed its peak' following Steve Jobs's death. He systematically rebuts each of Crow's claims — that Maps proves Apple lost its way, that the iPad 4 was an 'insipid update,' that Apple over-hypes incremental products, and that innovation died with Jobs. Gruber argues that the Maps switch was exactly what Jobs would have done given Google's competitive threat, that incremental year-over-year improvement IS Apple's winning formula, and that no Apple product — not even the iPhone or iPad — was universally praised at launch. He acknowledges the long-term verdict is out but finds the evidence so far unconvincing.
key_points: The iOS 6 Maps switch was quintessentially Apple — taking control of essential technology — and Jobs himself would have made the same call given Google's competitive leverage over turn-by-turn navigation

Critics retroactively treat Jobs's tenure as flawless while holding Cook to an impossible standard of perfection; the real virtue is recognizing and correcting mistakes, which Cook demonstrated by firing Browett and Forstall
Year-over-year incremental improvement is Apple's actual winning formula, not pulling revolutionary products out of a hat at every keynote
No major Apple product was universally hailed at launch — the iPad was dismissed as 'a big iPhone' and the original iPhone was literally laughed at by competitors
The collective desire for Apple to produce iPhone-level novelty at every event is unreasonable and ahistorical
money_quotes: Steve Jobs would have traded in Google Maps for a folded-up map from a gas station at this point, given the competitive situation between Apple and Google.
What you want is to be (1) right more often than wrong; (2) willing to recognize when you are wrong; and (3) able and willing to correct whatever is wrong.
The only way to come close to being right all the time is to be willing to change your mind and recognize mistakes — it's never going to happen that you're right all the time in the first place.
You're not selling me on the Google org structure, here, Dan.
Just because it's the best smartphone ever made (agrees Crow himself), doesn't mean Apple should sing its praises? Maybe I need to brush up on my marketing.
Year-over-year incremental improvement is Apple's winning formula.
No Apple product has ever been universally hailed when it launched.
Even the original iPhone was laughed at — literally laughed at and mocked — by competitors.
key_insight: The narrative that Apple is doomed without Jobs rests on retroactively treating his tenure as flawless and holding his successors to an impossible standard that Jobs himself never met.
